title: China PIPL Art.38 localization boundary monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: PIPL Art.38 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PIPL Art.38 (Cross-border transfer conditions) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.29.1: China PIPL Art.38 localization boundary monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.29.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails, DLP system outputs, consent management platforms, data classification tools, cross-border transfer logs, privacy impact assessments, breach notification records
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=db_audit sourcetype="oracle:audit" Action="SELECT" earliest=-36h
| stats count by DBUserName, Obj_Name, client_ip
| iplocation client_ip
| where Country!="China" AND match(Obj_Name,"(?i)resident")
| sort - count
description: Provides APAC privacy supervisory evidence for china pipl art.38 localization boundary monitoring aligned with PIPL Art.38; ASEAN CBPR.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PIPL compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes db_audit and sourcetypes oracle:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `apac_transfer_register.csv` and consent or DPIA lookups referenced in runbooks. (2) Map technical identifiers to records of processing activities with legal review. (3) Restrict indexes to regional privacy roles with least privilege. (4) Quarterly DPIA and consent linkage review with local counsel. Indexes required: `index=db_audit`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=oracle:audit`.
visualization: Stacked bar (jurisdiction x action), Table (open reviews), Single value (exports without mechanism). Key fields: `DBUserName`, `Obj_Name`, `client_ip`.

grandmaExplanation: We watch for provides apac privacy supervisory evidence for china pipl art.38 localization boundary monitoring aligned with pipl ; asean cbpr. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails, DLP system outputs, consent management platforms, data classification tools, cross-border transfer logs, privacy impact assessments, breach notification records.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `apac_transfer_register.csv` and consent or DPIA lookups referenced in runbooks. (2) Map technical identifiers to records of processing activities with legal review. (3) Restrict indexes to regional privacy roles with least privilege. (4) Quarterly DPIA and consent linkage review with local counsel.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=db_audit sourcetype="oracle:audit" Action="SELECT" earliest=-36h
| stats count by DBUserName, Obj_Name, client_ip
| iplocation client_ip
| where Country!="China" AND match(Obj_Name,"(?i)resident")
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**China PIPL Art.38 localization boundary monitoring (PIPL Art.38; ASEAN CBPR)** — Provides APAC privacy supervisory evidence for china pipl art.38 localization boundary monitoring aligned with PIPL Art.38; ASEAN CBPR.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails, DLP system outputs, consent management platforms, data classification tools, cross-border transfer logs, privacy impact assessments, breach notification records. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: db_audit; **sourcetype**: oracle:audit. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=db_audit, sourcetype="oracle:audit", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **DBUserName, Obj_Name, client_ip**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.

• Filters the current rows with `where Country!="China" AND match(Obj_Name,"(?i)resident")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Output fields: DBUserName, Obj_Name, client_ip.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked bar (jurisdiction x action), Table (open reviews), Single value (exports without mechanism).
